title: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure
authors: Kathleen D. Liu, Glenn M. Chertow

items.subtopic: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure
items.title: Understanding Dialysis Modalities for Acute vs. Chronic Kidney Disease
items.content: Dialysis is a critical treatment modality for both acute and chronic kidney disease (CKD). Continuous renal replacement therapies (CRRTs) and prolonged intermittent renal replacement therapy (PIRRT)/slow low-efficiency dialysis (SLED) are specifically used in the management of acute kidney injury/acute kidney disease. These modalities differ from standard intermittent hemodialysis by being performed continuously or over longer sessions, typically 6–12 hours.
keyPoints: CRRT and PIRRT/SLED are specific to acute kidney injury/acute kidney disease.
Performed continuously (CRRT) or over 6–12 hours per session (PIRRT/SLED).
Contrast with intermittent hemodialysis sessions of 3–4 hours.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure

items.subtopic: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure
items.title: Prevalence and Causes of End-Stage Kidney Disease (ESKD)
items.content: The prevalence of treated end-stage kidney disease (ESKD) has increased significantly, reflecting enhanced survival rates for patients on dialysis. In the United States, there are over 800,000 patients with treated ESKD, primarily requiring dialysis. The leading cause of ESKD is diabetes mellitus, accounting for approximately 45% of new cases. Other significant causes include hypertension (30%), glomerulonephritis, polycystic kidney disease, and obstructive uropathy.
keyPoints: Over 800,000 patients with treated ESKD in the United States.
Prevalence increased by 65% since 2000 due to enhanced survival on dialysis.
Crude incidence rate for treated ESKD is 363 cases per million population per year.
~45% of new ESKD cases are due to diabetes mellitus.
30% attributed to hypertension, with causality unclear.
ESKD disproportionately higher in African Americans.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure

items.subtopic: TREATMENT OPTIONS FOR PATIENTS WITH ESKD
items.title: Criteria for Initiating Maintenance Dialysis in ESKD
items.content: Maintenance dialysis should be initiated based on specific clinical criteria, including uremic symptoms, hyperkalemia unresponsive to conservative measures, persistent extracellular volume expansion despite diuretic therapy, acidosis refractory to medical therapy, bleeding diathesis, and a creatinine clearance or estimated GFR <10 mL/min per 1.73 m².
keyPoints: Initiate dialysis for uremic symptoms, hyperkalemia unresponsive to conservative measures, persistent extracellular volume expansion despite diuretic therapy, acidosis refractory to medical therapy, bleeding diathesis.
Creatinine clearance or estimated GFR <10 mL/min per 1.73 m² is a criterion.
No absolute level of serum creatinine, cystatin C, BUN, or estimated GFR mandates dialysis initiation.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · TREATMENT OPTIONS FOR PATIENTS WITH ESKD

items.subtopic: TREATMENT OPTIONS FOR PATIENTS WITH ESKD
items.title: Choosing Between Hemodialysis and Peritoneal Dialysis in ESKD
items.content: The choice between hemodialysis and peritoneal dialysis for treating ESKD often depends on personal preferences and quality-of-life considerations, as outcomes are similar with both modalities. In-center hemodialysis is the most common modality in the U.S., while peritoneal dialysis offers continuous treatment but less efficient solute clearance.
keyPoints: Hemodialysis (in-center or home) and peritoneal dialysis (CAPD, CCPD) are primary ESKD treatments.
>80% of patients in the U.S. undergo in-center hemodialysis.
No large-scale trials show significant outcome differences between hemodialysis and peritoneal dialysis.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · TREATMENT OPTIONS FOR PATIENTS WITH ESKD

items.title: Factors Influencing Solute Clearance in Hemodialysis
items.content: Solute clearance during hemodialysis is influenced by the concentration gradient, membrane surface area, and mass transfer coefficient. Larger molecules have slower diffusion rates across the dialysis membrane.
keyPoints: Diffusive transport rate increases with larger concentration gradients, greater membrane surface area, and higher mass transfer coefficients.
Mass transfer coefficient depends on membrane porosity, thickness, solute size, and flow conditions.
Urea (60 Da) is cleared more efficiently than creatinine (113 Da).
Convective clearance occurs due to solvent drag in ultrafiltration.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · HEMODIALYSIS | THE DIALYZER

items.title: Components and Evolution of Dialyzers in Hemodialysis
items.content: The dialyzer, a key component of hemodialysis, has evolved to use biocompatible synthetic membranes. The hollow-fiber design is prevalent, allowing simultaneous perfusion of blood and dialysate.
keyPoints: Dialyzers consist of plastic chambers with high flow rates for blood and dialysate compartments.
Hollow-fiber dialyzers are common in the U.S., using polysulfone or similar synthetic membranes.
These biocompatible membranes reduce complement cascade activation compared to older cellulose membranes.
Use of single-use dialyzers has increased as costs decrease, making reprocessing rare in the U.S.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · HEMODIALYSIS | THE DIALYZER

items.title: Optimizing Potassium Concentration in Dialysate to Reduce Sudden Death Risk
items.content: The potassium concentration of dialysate is adjusted based on the patient's predialysis serum potassium levels, typically ranging from 0–4 mmol/L. However, using very low potassium concentrations (0- or 1-mmol/L) in dialysate has been linked to an increased risk of sudden death due to arrhythmias caused by potassium shifts.
keyPoints: Potassium concentration in dialysate: 0–4 mmol/L
Low potassium dialysate (0- or 1-mmol/L) associated with increased risk of sudden death
Risk possibly due to arrhythmias from potassium shifts
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · DIALYSATE | BLOOD DELIVERY SYSTEM

items.title: Sodium Modeling in Dialysis: Balancing Hypotension and Sodium Balance
items.content: Sodium modeling is employed to manage hypotension during dialysis by adjusting the dialysate sodium concentration from 145–155 mmol/L to isotonic levels (135–140 mmol/L) towards the end of treatment. This approach helps counterbalance urea-related osmolar gradients but may lead to positive sodium balance and increased thirst, which can be problematic for patients with hypertension or large interdialytic weight gains.
keyPoints: Sodium modeling: Dialysate sodium concentration adjusted from 145–155 mmol/L to 135–140 mmol/L
Used to manage hypotension by counterbalancing urea-related osmolar gradients
Potential risks include positive sodium balance and increased thirst, especially in hypertensive patients or those with large interdialytic weight gains
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · DIALYSATE | BLOOD DELIVERY SYSTEM

items.title: Why Choose Fistulas Over Other Hemodialysis Access Options?
items.content: Fistulas, specifically arteriovenous fistulas like the Brescia-Cimino, are preferred for hemodialysis access due to their high long-term patency rates. They involve anastomosing an artery to a vein, leading to arterialization of the vein which supports large needle placement.
keyPoints: Fistulas have the highest long-term patency rate among hemodialysis access options.
Typically involves creating a Brescia-Cimino fistula by connecting the cephalic vein at the wrist end-to-side to the radial artery.
"Fistula first" initiative encourages creation of arteriovenous fistulas in more patients.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · DIALYSIS ACCESS

items.title: Complications and Management of Arteriovenous Grafts and Catheters
items.content: Arteriovenous grafts are prone to thrombosis due to intimal hyperplasia at the anastomosis, leading to graft failure. Monitoring venous pressures on dialysis and access flow can help in early recognition of vascular access issues. Tunneled catheters, while useful when fistulas or grafts fail, should be used cautiously due to higher infection rates.
keyPoints: Arteriovenous grafts have a high risk of thrombosis and failure primarily from intimal hyperplasia at the anastomosis.
Catheter-guided angioplasty can manage stenoses in failing grafts or fistulas.
Tunneled catheters, often placed in internal jugular veins, reduce infection rates compared to nontunneled catheters but should be used temporarily due to risks of venous thrombosis and stenosis.
Subclavian vein catheter placement is generally avoided due to high risk of stenosis.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · DIALYSIS ACCESS

items.title: Understanding Dialysis Adequacy Metrics
items.content: The adequacy of hemodialysis is traditionally measured by urea clearance metrics, specifically the urea reduction ratio (URR) and Kt/V. While these measures have been standard for assessing dialysis quality, recent studies like HEMO suggest that higher per-session urea clearance does not necessarily correlate with reduced mortality.
keyPoints: Urea reduction ratio target: >65–70%
Kt/V target: >1.2 or 1.05 depending on equilibration
HEMO study showed no mortality difference with higher per-session urea clearance
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · GOALS OF DIALYSIS

items.title: Impact of Hemodialysis Frequency on Patient Outcomes
items.content: More frequent hemodialysis sessions have been associated with improved clinical outcomes, including better control of hypertension and hyperphosphatemia, reduced left ventricular mass, and enhanced self-reported physical health. The Frequent Hemodialysis Network Daily Trial demonstrated these benefits when comparing 6 versus 3 weekly sessions.
keyPoints: Frequent Hemodialysis Network Daily Trial: improved outcomes with more frequent sessions
Benefits include better hypertension control, reduced left ventricular mass, and enhanced physical health
Increased mortality and heart failure hospitalization noted during longer interdialytic intervals (weekend)
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · GOALS OF DIALYSIS

items.title: Management of Hypotension During Hemodialysis
items.content: Hypotension is the most common acute complication during hemodialysis, especially in patients with diabetes mellitus. It can be caused by excessive ultrafiltration, impaired autonomic responses, osmolar shifts, and overuse of antihypertensives.
keyPoints: Discontinue ultrafiltration to manage hypotension.
Administer 100–250 mL of isotonic saline or salt-poor albumin (unavailable in outpatient settings).
Prevent by evaluating dry weight and using ultrafiltration modeling, removing more fluid at the beginning of dialysis.
Avoid rapid fluid removal (>13 mL/kg per h) to prevent adverse outcomes including cardiovascular deaths.
Use sequential ultrafiltration followed by dialysis, cool dialysate, and avoid heavy meals during treatment.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · COMPLICATIONS DURING HEMODIALYSIS

items.title: Understanding Anaphylactoid Reactions During Hemodialysis
items.content: Anaphylactoid reactions to the dialyzer can occur, particularly with first use. These are categorized into Type A and B reactions.
keyPoints: Type A reactions: IgE-mediated hypersensitivity due to ethylene oxide sterilization, occurring within minutes of treatment initiation.
Type B reactions: Nonspecific chest/back pain from complement activation and cytokine release, appearing several minutes into dialysis and resolving over time.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · COMPLICATIONS DURING HEMODIALYSIS

items.subtopic: PERITONEAL DIALYSIS | FORMS OF PERITONEAL DIALYSIS
items.title: Mechanisms of Solute and Fluid Clearance in Peritoneal Dialysis
items.content: Peritoneal dialysis involves the infusion of a dextrose-containing solution into the peritoneal cavity, which facilitates solute and fluid removal through convective and diffusive clearance. The process relies on ultrafiltration and concentration gradients to clear metabolic by-products.
keyPoints: 1.5–3 L of dextrose-containing solution is infused
Dwell time usually 2–4 hours
Clearance depends on movement into vs. absorption from the peritoneal cavity
Rate of diffusion diminishes over time until equilibration
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · PERITONEAL DIALYSIS | FORMS OF PERITONEAL DIALYSIS

items.subtopic: PERITONEAL DIALYSIS | FORMS OF PERITONEAL DIALYSIS
items.title: Forms and Optimization of Peritoneal Dialysis
items.content: Peritoneal dialysis can be performed as CAPD or CCPD, with manual exchanges during the day for CAPD and automated cycles at night for CCPD. The adequacy of solute clearance is tracked to ensure effective treatment, with solutions typically ranging from 1.5–3 L in volume.
keyPoints: CAPD involves manual exchanges three to five times daily
CCPD uses an automated cycler at night
Solution volumes range from 1.5–3 L
Icodextrin used for efficient ultrafiltration as the 'last fill'
Heparin and antibiotics may be added to prevent catheter obstruction or treat peritonitis
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · PERITONEAL DIALYSIS | FORMS OF PERITONEAL DIALYSIS

items.title: Importance of Catheter Design in Peritoneal Dialysis
items.content: The design and placement of peritoneal catheters are crucial for effective dialysis. Flexible silicone rubber catheters with side holes and two Dacron cuffs ensure secure anchoring and prevent bacterial contamination.
keyPoints: Catheters have numerous side holes at the distal end.
Two Dacron cuffs anchor the catheter and seal it from bacteria.
Cuffs are placed in the preperitoneal plane approximately 2 cm from the skin surface.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · ACCESS TO THE PERITONEAL CAVITY

items.title: Peritoneal Transport Characteristics and Dialysis Regimen
items.content: The peritoneal equilibrium test classifies patients by transport rates, influencing dialysis regimens. High transporters require more frequent exchanges with shorter dwell times, often necessitating a cycler.
keyPoints: Patients are classified as low, low-average, high-average, and high transporters based on the peritoneal equilibrium test.
High transporters absorb more glucose and lose efficiency of ultrafiltration with long daytime dwells.
Low and low-average transporters do well with fewer exchanges.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · ACCESS TO THE PERITONEAL CAVITY

items.title: Management of Peritonitis in Peritoneal Dialysis
items.content: Peritonitis is a major complication during peritoneal dialysis, often due to breaches in sterile technique. It's diagnosed by an elevated peritoneal fluid leukocyte count (≥100/mm³ with ≥50% polymorphonuclear neutrophils). Common pathogens include gram-positive cocci like Staphylococcus. Treatment typically involves intraperitoneal or oral antibiotics, but catheter removal is necessary for infections caused by hydrophilic gram-negative rods or yeast.
keyPoints: Peritonitis defined by leukocyte count ≥100/mm³ with ≥50% polymorphonuclear neutrophils
Common pathogens: Gram-positive cocci (e.g., Staphylococcus)
Treatment: Intraperitoneal/oral antibiotics; catheter removal for hydrophilic gram-negative rods or yeast
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · COMPLICATIONS DURING PERITONEAL DIALYSIS

items.title: Metabolic Complications of Peritoneal Dialysis
items.content: Peritoneal dialysis can lead to metabolic disturbances such as hypoproteinemia, hyperglycemia, and weight gain. Protein loss occurs through the peritoneal membrane, necessitating increased dietary protein intake. Hyperglycemia results from dextrose absorption, with several hundred calories absorbed daily depending on concentration. Continuous dialysis allows for a more liberal diet regarding potassium and phosphorus.
keyPoints: Protein loss leads to hypoproteinemia requiring higher dietary protein
Hyperglycemia due to dextrose absorption (several hundred calories/day)
Continuous removal of potassium and phosphorus allows for a more liberal diet
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · COMPLICATIONS DURING PERITONEAL DIALYSIS

items.subtopic: LONG-TERM OUTCOMES IN ESKD | GLOBAL PERSPECTIVE
items.title: Cardiovascular Disease as a Leading Cause of Death in ESKD
items.content: Cardiovascular disease is the primary cause of mortality among patients with end-stage kidney disease (ESKD). The rates of cardiovascular events and deaths are higher for those on dialysis compared to post-transplantation, though both groups experience elevated risks. Shared risk factors such as diabetes mellitus, hypertension, and chronic inflammation contribute to this high incidence.
keyPoints: Cardiovascular disease is the major cause of death in ESKD patients.
Higher cardiovascular mortality rates are observed in dialysis patients compared to post-transplantation patients.
Shared risk factors include diabetes mellitus, hypertension, and chronic inflammation.
No consistent benefit from targeted cardiovascular risk reduction studies in ESKD has been demonstrated.
SHARP study showed a 17% reduction in major cardiovascular events or death with simvastatin-ezetimibe treatment.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · LONG-TERM OUTCOMES IN ESKD | GLOBAL PERSPECTIVE

items.subtopic: LONG-TERM OUTCOMES IN ESKD | GLOBAL PERSPECTIVE
items.title: Cardioprotective Strategies and Emerging Therapies for ESKD Patients
items.content: Conventional cardioprotective strategies are recommended for dialysis patients based on their elevated cardiovascular risk profile. These include lipid-lowering agents, aspirin, renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system inhibitors, and beta-adrenergic antagonists. Emerging therapies such as SGLT-2 inhibitors and GLP-1 receptor agonists have not yet been formally studied for cardiovascular risk reduction in ESKD.
keyPoints: Conventional cardioprotective strategies are recommended based on increased cardiovascular risk in dialysis patients.
Strategies include lipid-lowering agents, aspirin, renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system inhibitors, and beta-adrenergic antagonists.
Cardiovascular risk is more than an order of magnitude higher in ESKD patients compared to those without kidney disease.
SGLT-2 inhibitors and GLP-1 receptor agonists have not been formally studied for cardiovascular risk reduction in ESKD.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · LONG-TERM OUTCOMES IN ESKD | GLOBAL PERSPECTIVE

items.subtopic: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure
items.question: Which dialysis modality is rarely used for acute kidney injury/acute kidney disease in developed countries due to increased risk of infection?
options: Prolonged intermittent renal replacement therapy (PIRRT)/slow low-efficiency dialysis (SLED)
items.explanation: Peritoneal dialysis is rarely used in developed countries for acute kidney injury due to increased risk of infection.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure

items.subtopic: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure
items.question: What is the leading cause of end-stage kidney disease (ESKD) in the United States?
items.explanation: Diabetes mellitus is the leading cause of ESKD in the United States, accounting for ~45% of newly diagnosed cases.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure

items.subtopic: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure
items.question: Which factor has contributed to the increased prevalence of treated end-stage kidney disease (ESKD) since 2000?
options: Decreased survival rates among patients receiving dialysis
Enhanced survival of patients receiving dialysis
items.explanation: The increased prevalence of treated ESKD since 2000 predominantly reflects enhanced survival of patients receiving dialysis.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure

items.subtopic: TREATMENT OPTIONS FOR PATIENTS WITH ESKD
items.question: Which of the following is NOT a commonly accepted criterion for initiating maintenance dialysis in patients with ESKD?
options: Creatinine clearance or estimated GFR <10 mL/min per 1.73 m²
Persistent extracellular volume expansion despite diuretic therapy
items.explanation: Initiation of maintenance dialysis is considered when hyperkalemia is unresponsive, not responsive, to conservative measures.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · TREATMENT OPTIONS FOR PATIENTS WITH ESKD

items.subtopic: TREATMENT OPTIONS FOR PATIENTS WITH ESKD
items.question: What percentage of patients with ESKD in the United States primarily use in-center hemodialysis?
items.explanation: In-center hemodialysis is used by more than 80% of patients with ESKD in the United States.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · TREATMENT OPTIONS FOR PATIENTS WITH ESKD

items.subtopic: TREATMENT OPTIONS FOR PATIENTS WITH ESKD
items.question: Which dialysis modality is described as continuous but less efficient in terms of solute clearance?
items.explanation: Peritoneal dialysis, including CAPD, is continuous but less efficient in solute clearance compared to hemodialysis.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · TREATMENT OPTIONS FOR PATIENTS WITH ESKD

items.question: Which factor does NOT increase the rate of diffusive transport in hemodialysis?
options: The magnitude of the concentration gradient
The mass transfer coefficient of the membrane
The size of the solute molecule
items.explanation: The rate of diffusive transport decreases with larger solute molecules.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · HEMODIALYSIS | THE DIALYZER

items.question: Which type of dialyzer is most commonly used in the United States?
items.explanation: The hollow-fiber dialyzer made from polysulfone or related compounds is most common in the United States.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · HEMODIALYSIS | THE DIALYZER

items.question: What type of membranes are currently used for dialyzers manufactured in the United States?
items.explanation: Dialyzers in the United States use polysulfone biocompatible synthetic membranes.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · HEMODIALYSIS | THE DIALYZER

items.question: What is a potential risk associated with using very low potassium concentrations (0-1 mmol/L) in the dialysate?
options: Increased risk of sudden death due to arrhythmias
items.explanation: Patients treated with very low potassium dialysate have an increased risk of sudden death, possibly due to arrhythmias.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · DIALYSATE | BLOOD DELIVERY SYSTEM

items.question: In which scenario might higher dialysate calcium concentrations be used?
options: Patients experiencing frequent hypotension during dialysis
Patients with hypocalcemia or secondary hyperparathyroidism
items.explanation: Higher dialysate calcium concentrations may be used in patients with hypocalcemia or associated with secondary hyperparathyroidism.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · DIALYSATE | BLOOD DELIVERY SYSTEM

items.question: What is the purpose of sodium modeling during dialysis?
options: To increase potassium levels in patients
To prevent sudden death due to arrhythmias
To counterbalance urea-related osmolar gradients and manage hypotension
To enhance ultrafiltration by manipulating hydrostatic pressure
items.explanation: Sodium modeling is employed to counterbalance urea-related osmolar gradients, helping manage intradialytic hypotension.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · DIALYSATE | BLOOD DELIVERY SYSTEM

items.question: Which type of hemodialysis access has the highest long-term patency rate?
items.explanation: Fistulas have the highest long-term patency rate of all hemodialysis access options.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · DIALYSIS ACCESS

items.question: What is a common complication associated with arteriovenous grafts?
options: Thrombosis due to intimal hyperplasia at the anastomosis
items.explanation: The most important complication of arteriovenous grafts is thrombosis of the graft and graft failure, due principally to intimal hyperplasia at the anastomosis between the graft and recipient vein.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · DIALYSIS ACCESS

items.question: Why are tunneled catheters preferred over nontunneled temporary catheters?
options: They reduce bacterial translocation from the skin
They can be used in any vein
items.explanation: Tunneled catheters are often used because they are tunneled under the skin, which reduces bacterial translocation from the skin, resulting in a lower infection rate than with nontunneled temporary catheters.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · DIALYSIS ACCESS

items.question: Which of the following is a standard method by which 'adequacy of dialysis' is measured?
options: Blood flow rate through the dialyzer
items.explanation: The fractional removal of urea nitrogen is considered a standard method for measuring 'adequacy of dialysis'.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · GOALS OF DIALYSIS

items.question: What did the HEMO study fail to show regarding per-session urea clearance?
options: A significant improvement in patient health
items.explanation: The HEMO study failed to show a difference in mortality associated with a large difference in per-session urea clearance.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · GOALS OF DIALYSIS

items.question: What is the target urea reduction ratio for hemodialysis sessions?
items.explanation: The current targets include a urea reduction ratio of >65–70% per hemodialysis session.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · GOALS OF DIALYSIS

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a common cause of hypotension during hemodialysis?
options: Excessive ultrafiltration with inadequate compensatory vascular filling
items.explanation: Reduced, not increased, cardiac reserve is a risk factor for hypotension during hemodialysis.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · COMPLICATIONS DURING HEMODIALYSIS

items.question: What is the recommended initial management of hypotension during dialysis?
options: Discontinuing ultrafiltration and administering 100–250 mL of isotonic saline
Increasing the rate of ultrafiltration to stabilize blood pressure
items.explanation: Discontinuing ultrafiltration and administering isotonic saline is recommended for managing hypotension.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · COMPLICATIONS DURING HEMODIALYSIS

items.question: Which of the following strategies can help prevent dialysis-associated muscle cramps?
options: Increasing ultrafiltration rate to remove more fluid quickly
items.explanation: Reducing volume removal can help prevent muscle cramps associated with dialysis.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · COMPLICATIONS DURING HEMODIALYSIS

items.subtopic: PERITONEAL DIALYSIS | FORMS OF PERITONEAL DIALYSIS
items.question: Which of the following best describes the primary mechanism by which hypertonic peritoneal dialysis solutions facilitate solute and fluid removal?
options: Diffusive clearance down a concentration gradient
Hypertonicity driving osmotic movement into the peritoneal cavity
items.explanation: The hypertonicity of peritoneal dialysis solutions drives solute and fluid removal by creating an osmotic gradient.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · PERITONEAL DIALYSIS | FORMS OF PERITONEAL DIALYSIS

items.subtopic: PERITONEAL DIALYSIS | FORMS OF PERITONEAL DIALYSIS
items.question: What is the primary purpose of using icodextrin in peritoneal dialysis?
options: To prevent obstruction of the dialysis catheter lumen with fibrin
To provide a nonabsorbable carbohydrate for more efficient ultrafiltration
To add amino acids to address protein-energy malnutrition
To serve as an antibiotic during acute peritonitis
items.explanation: Icodextrin is used because it provides more efficient ultrafiltration compared to dextrose-containing solutions.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · PERITONEAL DIALYSIS | FORMS OF PERITONEAL DIALYSIS

items.subtopic: PERITONEAL DIALYSIS | FORMS OF PERITONEAL DIALYSIS
items.question: Which form of peritoneal dialysis involves automated exchanges typically performed at night?
items.explanation: CCPD (Continuous Cycling Peritoneal Dialysis) involves automated exchanges typically performed at night.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · PERITONEAL DIALYSIS | FORMS OF PERITONEAL DIALYSIS

items.question: What material are maintenance peritoneal dialysis catheters typically made from?
items.explanation: Maintenance peritoneal dialysis catheters are typically made of silicone rubber.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · ACCESS TO THE PERITONEAL CAVITY

items.question: What is the primary purpose of the Dacron cuffs on a peritoneal dialysis catheter?
options: To increase fluid flow through the catheter
To anchor the catheter and prevent bacterial infection
To measure the transfer rates of creatinine and glucose
To allow for larger volumes of dialysate infusion
items.explanation: The Dacron cuffs scarring around them anchors the catheter and seals it from bacteria tracking into the peritoneal cavity.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · ACCESS TO THE PERITONEAL CAVITY

items.question: Which patients require more frequent, shorter dwell-time exchanges in peritoneal dialysis?
items.explanation: High transporters tend to absorb more glucose and lose efficiency of ultrafiltration, requiring more frequent, shorter dwell-time exchanges.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · ACCESS TO THE PERITONEAL CAVITY

items.question: Which organism is most commonly responsible for peritonitis during peritoneal dialysis?
items.explanation: Peritonitis in peritoneal dialysis is most commonly caused by gram-positive cocci, such as Staphylococcus.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · COMPLICATIONS DURING PERITONEAL DIALYSIS

items.question: What is a common metabolic complication associated with peritoneal dialysis?
items.explanation: Peritoneal dialysis can lead to hypoproteinemia due to the loss of albumin and other proteins across the peritoneal membrane.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · COMPLICATIONS DURING PERITONEAL DIALYSIS

items.question: What is often required for complete eradication of infection in cases of peritonitis caused by hydrophilic gram-negative rods?
items.explanation: For peritonitis caused by hydrophilic gram-negative rods, catheter removal is usually required to ensure complete eradication of infection.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · COMPLICATIONS DURING PERITONEAL DIALYSIS

items.subtopic: LONG-TERM OUTCOMES IN ESKD | GLOBAL PERSPECTIVE
items.question: What is the major cause of death in patients with end-stage kidney disease (ESKD)?
items.explanation: Cardiovascular disease constitutes the major cause of death in ESKD.

items.question: Which study demonstrated a reduction in cardiovascular events with simvastatin-ezetimibe treatment?
items.explanation: The SHARP study showed a 17% reduction in major cardiovascular events or cardiovascular death with simvastatin-ezetimibe treatment.

items.question: Which agents have not been formally studied for cardiovascular risk reduction in the context of ESKD?
items.explanation: To date, SGLT-2 inhibitors have not been formally studied for cardiovascular risk reduction in the context of ESKD.

items.subtopic: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure
items.statement: The crude incidence rate for treated end-stage kidney disease (ESKD) in the United States is increasing.
items.explanation: The incidence rate for treated ESKD has slowly fallen over time.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure

items.subtopic: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure
items.statement: Mortality rates for patients with end-stage kidney disease (ESKD) are highest in Europe and Japan.
items.explanation: Mortality rates for ESKD are lowest in Europe and Japan.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure

items.subtopic: TREATMENT OPTIONS FOR PATIENTS WITH ESKD
items.statement: There are large-scale clinical trials comparing outcomes between patients randomized to either hemodialysis or peritoneal dialysis.
items.explanation: No large-scale clinical trials have been completed comparing outcomes among patients randomized to either hemodialysis or peritoneal dialysis.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · TREATMENT OPTIONS FOR PATIENTS WITH ESKD

items.subtopic: TREATMENT OPTIONS FOR PATIENTS WITH ESKD
items.statement: Initiating dialysis preemptively at a GFR of 10–14 mL/min per 1.73 m² provides benefits compared to initiating for symptoms.
items.explanation: There is no benefit to initiating dialysis preemptively at a GFR of 10–14 mL/min per 1.73 m² compared to initiating it for uremic symptoms.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · TREATMENT OPTIONS FOR PATIENTS WITH ESKD

items.statement: Convective clearance occurs due to solvent drag, with solutes being swept along with water across the semipermeable dialysis membrane.
items.explanation: Convective clearance is indeed caused by solvent drag.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · HEMODIALYSIS | THE DIALYZER

items.statement: Reprocessing of hemodialyzers in the United States is common due to cost-effectiveness.
items.explanation: In the U.S., reprocessing of dialyzers is extremely rare.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · HEMODIALYSIS | THE DIALYZER

items.statement: The usual dialysate sodium concentration is 135–140 mmol/L.
items.explanation: The typical range for dialysate sodium concentration is indeed 135–140 mmol/L.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · DIALYSATE | BLOOD DELIVERY SYSTEM

items.statement: Water used in the dialysate undergoes reverse osmosis to remove microbiologic contaminants and dissolved ions.
items.explanation: The water for dialysate is subjected to filtration, softening, deionization, and reverse osmosis.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · DIALYSATE | BLOOD DELIVERY SYSTEM

items.statement: Subclavian veins are preferred for catheter placement due to excellent flow rates.
items.explanation: Nephrologists, interventional radiologists, and vascular surgeons generally prefer to avoid placement of catheters into the subclavian veins because subclavian stenosis is a frequent complication.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · DIALYSIS ACCESS

items.statement: Fistulas are associated with higher rates of infection compared to grafts and catheters.
items.explanation: Grafts and (in particular) catheters are associated with much higher rates of infection than fistulas.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · DIALYSIS ACCESS

items.statement: Longer hemodialysis sessions may be beneficial independent of urea clearance.
items.explanation: Studies suggest that longer hemodialysis session lengths may offer benefits beyond urea clearance.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · GOALS OF DIALYSIS

items.statement: The Frequent Hemodialysis Network Daily Trial showed no improvement in self-reported physical health with more frequent dialysis.
items.explanation: The trial demonstrated improved self-reported physical health with more frequent hemodialysis.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · GOALS OF DIALYSIS

items.statement: Excessively rapid fluid removal during hemodialysis is associated with adverse outcomes, including cardiovascular deaths.
items.explanation: Rapid fluid removal (>13 mL/kg per h) has been linked to adverse outcomes.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · COMPLICATIONS DURING HEMODIALYSIS

items.statement: Type A dialyzer reactions are caused by complement activation and cytokine release.
items.explanation: Type A reactions are IgE-mediated hypersensitivity to ethylene oxide, not complement activation.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · COMPLICATIONS DURING HEMODIALYSIS

items.subtopic: PERITONEAL DIALYSIS | FORMS OF PERITONEAL DIALYSIS
items.statement: The rate of peritoneal solute transport is uniform across all patients.
items.explanation: The rate of peritoneal solute transport varies from patient to patient.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · PERITONEAL DIALYSIS | FORMS OF PERITONEAL DIALYSIS

items.subtopic: PERITONEAL DIALYSIS | FORMS OF PERITONEAL DIALYSIS
items.statement: Icodextrin is typically used as the 'last fill' for patients on CCPD.
items.explanation: Icodextrin is commonly used as the last fill in CCPD to enhance ultrafiltration.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · PERITONEAL DIALYSIS | FORMS OF PERITONEAL DIALYSIS

items.statement: The peritoneal equilibrium test classifies patients into four categories based on their transport characteristics.
items.explanation: Patients are classified as low, low-average, high-average, and high transporters.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · ACCESS TO THE PERITONEAL CAVITY

items.statement: The ADEMEX trial demonstrated a significant reduction in mortality with increased urea clearance.
items.explanation: The ADEMEX trial failed to show a significant reduction in mortality or complications with increased urea clearance.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · ACCESS TO THE PERITONEAL CAVITY

items.statement: Most cases of peritonitis during peritoneal dialysis require hospitalization.
items.explanation: Many patients with peritonitis do not require hospitalization and can be managed with intraperitoneal or oral antibiotics.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · COMPLICATIONS DURING PERITONEAL DIALYSIS

items.statement: Continuous nature of peritoneal dialysis allows for a more liberal diet due to continuous removal of potassium and phosphorus.
items.explanation: The continuous nature of peritoneal dialysis facilitates the removal of potassium and phosphorus, allowing for a more liberal diet.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · COMPLICATIONS DURING PERITONEAL DIALYSIS

items.subtopic: LONG-TERM OUTCOMES IN ESKD | GLOBAL PERSPECTIVE
items.statement: Cardiovascular mortality rates are lower in patients receiving dialysis compared to those posttransplantation.
items.explanation: Cardiovascular mortality and event rates are higher in patients receiving dialysis than in those posttransplantation.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · LONG-TERM OUTCOMES IN ESKD | GLOBAL PERSPECTIVE

items.subtopic: LONG-TERM OUTCOMES IN ESKD | GLOBAL PERSPECTIVE
items.statement: The incidence of end-stage kidney disease is decreasing worldwide.
items.explanation: The incidence of ESKD is increasing worldwide with longer life expectancies and improved care.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · LONG-TERM OUTCOMES IN ESKD | GLOBAL PERSPECTIVE

items.assertion: The rate of diffusive transport increases with larger solute molecules.
items.reason: Larger molecules have a slower rate of transfer across the membrane.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; larger molecules diffuse more slowly.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · HEMODIALYSIS | THE DIALYZER

items.assertion: Higher dialysate sodium concentrations may predispose patients to positive sodium balance.
items.reason: This can lead to increased thirst and is undesirable in patients with hypertension or large interdialytic weight gains.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why higher dialysate sodium concentrations may be problematic.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · DIALYSATE | BLOOD DELIVERY SYSTEM

items.assertion: Tunneled catheters may be the last 'lifeline' for hemodialysis in patients with multiple vascular access complications.
items.reason: For patients with no other options for permanent vascular access, tunneled catheters provide necessary hemodialysis access.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation of the assertion.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · DIALYSIS ACCESS

items.assertion: Midodrine is routinely used for preventing hypotension during hemodialysis.
items.reason: There is insufficient evidence of its safety and efficacy to support the routine use of Midodrine.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because there's insufficient evidence for Midodrine's routine use.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · COMPLICATIONS DURING HEMODIALYSIS

items.assertion: Peritonitis is usually defined by an elevated peritoneal fluid leukocyte count.
items.reason: The cutoffs for defining peritonitis are lower than in spontaneous bacterial peritonitis due to the presence of dextrose and rapid bacterial proliferation.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: Dialysis in the Treatment of Kidney Failure · COMPLICATIONS DURING PERITONEAL DIALYSIS
